title: Medical Debt and Bankruptcy: What You Need to Know
excerpt: Medical bills are the leading cause of bankruptcy in America. Learn when bankruptcy might be the right option and what it means for your medical debt.
metaDescription: Considering bankruptcy for medical debt? Learn Chapter 7 vs Chapter 13, what happens to your bills, qualifying requirements, and alternatives to try first.

content.text: Medical debt is the leading cause of personal bankruptcy in America, contributing to over 60% of all bankruptcy filings. If you're overwhelmed by medical bills, bankruptcy might provide a fresh start. Here's what you need to know.
Medical Debt in Bankruptcy

Medical bills are classified as unsecured debt in bankruptcy – the same category as credit cards. This means they can be discharged (eliminated) through bankruptcy. You don't need a special 'medical bankruptcy' – regular bankruptcy handles medical debt.
Chapter 7 Bankruptcy

Chapter 7 eliminates most unsecured debt, including medical bills, in 3-6 months. You may have to give up some assets, but most people can keep their home, car, and retirement accounts. You must pass a means test based on income to qualify.
Chapter 13 Bankruptcy

Chapter 13 creates a 3-5 year repayment plan. Medical debt is included in the plan and any unpaid balance is discharged at the end. This option lets you keep all your assets but requires regular income to make plan payments.
Alternatives to Explore First

Before filing bankruptcy, explore: hospital financial assistance programs, negotiating reduced balances, payment plans, debt settlement with collectors, state assistance programs. Bankruptcy should be a last resort after other options are exhausted.
Impact on Your Credit

Bankruptcy stays on your credit report for 7-10 years and significantly impacts your credit score initially. However, for someone already drowning in medical debt and collections, bankruptcy can actually help rebuild credit faster by eliminating debt.
Consulting a Bankruptcy Attorney

Bankruptcy law is complex, and the decision to file is significant. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney – many offer free consultations. They can evaluate your specific situation, explain your options, and help you understand whether bankruptcy makes sense for you.
When Medical Debt Drives the Bankruptcy Decision

Medical debt is the leading cause of personal bankruptcy in the United States, accounting for an estimated 62 percent of filings according to studies published in the American Journal of Medicine. The combination of unexpected illness, lost income during illness, and high out-of-pocket costs creates financial crises that cannot be resolved through normal debt management tools. For patients in this situation, bankruptcy provides a legal mechanism to discharge medical debt and rebuild financially.

Medical debt is an unsecured debt — it is not backed by any collateral. This means it is dischargeable in bankruptcy under Chapter 7 and Chapter 13, unlike certain other debt types such as student loans (generally not dischargeable), child support (not dischargeable), and secured debt like mortgages (dischargeable but the collateral may still be at risk). The dischargeability of medical debt is one of the reasons bankruptcy can be a more complete solution for patients whose primary financial problem is medical bills rather than a mix of debt types.
Chapter 7 Bankruptcy for Medical Debt

Chapter 7 bankruptcy is a liquidation process that discharges most unsecured debt, including medical bills, within three to six months of filing. To qualify, you must pass the means test — a comparison of your income to the median income in your state. If your income is below the state median, you automatically qualify. If your income is above the median, a more detailed calculation determines whether you have sufficient disposable income to make payments under Chapter 13 instead.

Under Chapter 7, a bankruptcy trustee examines your assets and may liquidate non-exempt assets to pay creditors. However, state exemption laws protect core assets from liquidation — most states exempt your primary residence up to a certain value, one vehicle up to a certain value, household goods and clothing, retirement accounts, and tools of your trade. For patients whose primary debt is medical and whose assets are modest, Chapter 7 often results in full discharge with no liquidation of assets.
Chapter 13 Bankruptcy for Medical Debt

Chapter 13 bankruptcy allows you to repay a portion of your debt through a three-to-five-year repayment plan while discharging the remainder. Unsecured creditors including medical bill holders typically receive a fraction of the amount owed — often 10-30 cents on the dollar — through the plan. The remainder is discharged at plan completion. Chapter 13 is available to patients whose income exceeds the Chapter 7 means test threshold or who have non-exempt assets they want to protect.

Chapter 13 also provides the "cramdown" option for certain secured debts and the ability to catch up on mortgage arrears over the plan period — making it useful for patients who have both significant medical debt and a home they are at risk of losing due to missed payments during illness. The complexity of Chapter 13 generally requires attorney assistance — the plan must be feasible, the payments must be sustainable, and the paperwork is substantial.
The Long-Term Credit Impact of Bankruptcy

Chapter 7 bankruptcy remains on your credit report for ten years from the filing date. Chapter 13 remains for seven years. During this period, obtaining new credit, housing, or employment in certain fields may be more difficult. However, many patients who file bankruptcy report that their credit score begins improving within 12-18 months of the discharge as negative individual accounts are replaced by the single bankruptcy notation and they begin rebuilding with new credit.

The long-term credit impact should be weighed against the alternative: medical collections and judgments that also damage your credit but do not provide the fresh start that bankruptcy offers. For patients with $50,000 or more in medical debt and no realistic path to repayment, the credit impact of bankruptcy is often a better outcome than years of collection activity, potential wage garnishment, and the financial weight of unresolvable debt. Consult with a bankruptcy attorney for a free consultation to evaluate your specific situation.
Medical Debt Bankruptcy: Frequently Asked Questions

Patients considering bankruptcy for medical debt frequently ask the same questions. Can I keep my home? In most states, yes — the homestead exemption protects your primary residence up to a certain value (ranging from $25,000 in some states to unlimited in Texas and Florida). Can I keep my car? Typically yes, up to the exemption amount for your state (usually $2,500-$5,000). Will I lose my retirement accounts? No — 401(k), IRA, and pension accounts are exempt from bankruptcy liquidation under federal and state law. Can I file without an attorney? Technically yes, but Chapter 7 has enough complexity that professional help reduces errors significantly.

The automatic stay is one of the most immediate and powerful bankruptcy protections. When you file bankruptcy, the automatic stay immediately halts all collection activity — including collection calls, lawsuits, wage garnishments, and bank levies — on all debts covered by the filing. For patients already facing wage garnishment on medical debt judgments, the automatic stay provides immediate relief from income loss while the bankruptcy proceeds.
Alternatives to Bankruptcy for Medical Debt

Before filing bankruptcy, exhaust the alternatives specific to medical debt. Apply for hospital financial assistance programs — these can eliminate large balances entirely without the credit and legal consequences of bankruptcy. Negotiate directly with medical creditors using hardship documentation and fair market pricing data. Explore state and local medical debt relief programs. Contact nonprofit patient advocacy organizations that negotiate on behalf of patients.

Bankruptcy makes sense when the medical debt is too large for these alternatives to meaningfully address, when legal enforcement (wage garnishment, bank levies) is already occurring or imminent, when the debt is affecting your ability to meet basic living expenses, or when you have multiple large debt types in addition to medical bills that make a comprehensive fresh start more practical than piecemeal negotiation. The decision is personal and financial — a bankruptcy attorney can provide a realistic assessment of whether the outcome justifies the process.
Rebuilding After Medical Debt Bankruptcy

Credit rebuilding after bankruptcy begins immediately. After discharge, apply for a secured credit card (where you deposit money as collateral) and use it for small purchases you pay in full each month. Become an authorized user on a family member account with a long, positive history. After 12-18 months of positive payment history, apply for an unsecured credit card. Within two to three years of disciplined credit use, many bankruptcy filers reach credit scores in the 600-650 range — sufficient for most routine lending.

Also address the financial circumstances that led to the medical debt. If you were uninsured, obtain coverage through your employer, the ACA marketplace, or Medicaid. If you had inadequate coverage, evaluate your plan options during the next open enrollment period. If the medical event is ongoing, apply for financial assistance programs proactively before future bills accumulate. Bankruptcy provides the fresh start — the work of building financial stability after it requires both credit rebuilding and preventing the same situation from recurring.
Finding Affordable Bankruptcy Help for Medical Debt

Bankruptcy attorney fees typically range from $1,000-$2,000 for Chapter 7 and $2,500-$5,000 for Chapter 13, depending on the complexity of your case and local market rates. For patients with limited income, these fees can be a barrier. Several resources offer lower-cost or free bankruptcy assistance: Legal aid organizations provide free bankruptcy help to income-qualifying clients. Law school bankruptcy clinics handle cases under attorney supervision at no cost. Some bankruptcy attorneys offer payment plans or take fees from the bankruptcy estate in Chapter 13 cases.

The bankruptcy filing fee is $338 for Chapter 7 and $313 for Chapter 13 as of 2024. Courts may waive the filing fee for Chapter 7 filers whose income is below 150 percent of the federal poverty level. Apply for the fee waiver (Official Form 103B) when you file. Even if you cannot obtain free attorney help, filing pro se (without an attorney) is possible for Chapter 7 cases that are straightforward — the bankruptcy court website for your district provides forms, instructions, and resources for pro se filers.

Medical debt bankruptcy is not a failure — it is a legal tool specifically designed for situations where the financial burden of illness exceeds what a person can reasonably resolve through other means. The system exists because legislators recognized that medical debt is categorically different from consumer debt: it is unplanned, often unavoidable, and its scale can be catastrophic relative to income. For patients who have exhausted other options and face unmanageable medical debt, bankruptcy provides a legal mechanism to discharge the debt, stop collection activity, and rebuild. The decision should be made carefully and with legal guidance — but it should be made without stigma. It is what the law provides.

Medical debt bankruptcy is one of the most misunderstood options available to patients with overwhelming healthcare bills. The stigma attached to it is out of proportion to the reality: it is a legal process, available to anyone who qualifies, that resolves debt that cannot otherwise be resolved and provides a structured path to financial recovery. The patients who benefit most from it are those who explore it as a legitimate option rather than dismissing it as a last resort. For patients with large medical debt and limited assets, a bankruptcy consultation is one of the most valuable uses of an hour.

The decision to file bankruptcy should be made with complete information: what debts would be discharged, what assets are at risk, what the credit impact will be, and what alternatives remain. For patients whose primary financial problem is medical debt, bankruptcy often produces a better outcome than years of collection activity, potential wage garnishment, and the psychological weight of debt that cannot be paid. The consultation is free, the evaluation is private, and the decision is yours. If your medical bills have crossed the threshold where payment is genuinely impossible, bankruptcy exists precisely for that situation.

content.text: Medical debt bankruptcy is a last resort in the best sense: it is there when everything else has been tried and failed, and it works. The discharge is real, the automatic stay is immediate, and the fresh start is genuine. The patients who benefit from bankruptcy for medical debt are not those who gave up — they are those who exhausted every other option first and then used the legal tool designed precisely for their situation. There is no shame in that. There is only the practical question of whether the outcome justifies the process, and for many patients with large medical balances, it does.
